questions.question: Explain the structure of a Planning Graph and its two types of levels.
keyPoints: Planning Graph consists of alternating propositional levels (Pi) and action levels (Ai)
Propositional levels contain literals that could be true at that step
Action levels contain actions whose preconditions are satisfied by previous level
NOOP actions propagate persistent literals between levels

questions.question: Define mutex relation and explain any two types of mutex relations between actions.
keyPoints: Mutex (mutual exclusion) indicates elements that cannot coexist in the same plan step
Inconsistent Effects: one action adds literal p while another adds ¬p
Interference: one action deletes a precondition needed by another
Competing Needs: actions require mutually exclusive preconditions
Inverse: actions are exact opposites of each other

questions.question: Describe the GraphPlan algorithm with emphasis on its two main phases.
keyPoints: GraphPlan has two phases: expansion and solution extraction
Expansion phase: iteratively build planning graph by adding propositional and action levels
Solution extraction: backward search from goals to find non-mutex action sequence
Algorithm terminates when goals achieved or graph levels off

questions.question: What is graph leveling in Planning Graphs? When does it indicate failure?
keyPoints: Leveling occurs when consecutive propositional levels (Pi and Pi+1) are identical
This means no new literals can be added by further expansion
If goals are not present in the leveled-off graph, no solution exists
GraphPlan terminates unsuccessfully when it levels off without achieving goals

questions.question: Explain the concept of NOOP actions in Planning Graphs.
keyPoints: NOOP (no-operation) is a special dummy action
It maintains literals from one propositional level to the next without changes
Used to represent persistence assumptions in planning
Allows tracking of literals that are not affected by any action at a particular step

questions.question: How is a solution extracted from a Planning Graph in GraphPlan?
keyPoints: Solution extraction uses backward search from goal literals
Select actions that can achieve goals ensuring no mutex links
Recursively solve for preconditions of selected actions
Process continues until initial state level is reached
If at any level goals cannot be achieved without mutex, backtrack

questions.question: Compare Planning Graphs with traditional state-space search approaches.
keyPoints: Planning Graphs provide structured intermediate representation
Capture mutex information to avoid conflicting states
More efficient as they avoid irrelevant state exploration
State-space search can be exhaustive while Planning Graphs are more focused
However, Planning Graphs may miss some solutions due to incomplete representation

questions.question: Explain the four types of mutex relations between actions in Planning Graphs with examples.
keyPoints: Inconsistent Effects: action A adds p, action B adds ¬p (e.g., TurnOn adds LightOn, TurnOff adds LightOff)
Interference: action A deletes precondition of action B (e.g., PickUp deletes HandEmpty needed by PutDown)
Competing Needs: actions require mutually exclusive preconditions (e.g., Stack(A,B) needs On(A,C), Stack(B,A) needs On(B,C))
Inverse: actions are exact opposites (e.g., PickUp and Drop are inverses)
Each type prevents simultaneous execution of conflicting actions

questions.question: Describe the GraphPlan algorithm in detail including expansion and solution extraction phases.
keyPoints: GraphPlan builds Planning Graph iteratively starting from initial state
Expansion Phase: Add action level (actions with satisfied preconditions) and propositional level (effects of actions)
Compute mutex relations at each level to detect conflicts
Solution Extraction: Backward search from goal literals
At each level, select actions achieving goals ensuring no mutex
Recursively solve for preconditions until reaching initial level
Termination: Success if goals achievable without mutex, Failure if graph levels off without goals

questions.question: Discuss the advantages and limitations of Planning Graphs in AI planning.
keyPoints: Advantages: Efficient by capturing mutex information, avoids exploring conflicting states, provides structured representation, polynomial time complexity, guides plan search effectively

Limitations: May not find solutions for all solvable problems (incomplete representation), handles only propositional planning, may be conservative in mutex computation, exponential solution extraction in worst case
Practical use: Foundation for later algorithms like FF (Fast-Forward) and CPA (Critical Path Access)

questions.question: Explain the termination conditions of GraphPlan and how success/failure is determined.
keyPoints: Success condition: Goals appear in propositional level Pi with no mutex links between goal literals
Solution can be extracted by backward search from this level
Failure condition: Graph levels off (Pi = Pi+1) without achieving goals
Leveling indicates no new literals can be discovered
If goals not present in leveled-off graph, no solution exists
GraphPlan returns failure when both goals absent and graph has leveled off
